frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Priya, bura mat maanna, par aajkal zamaana theek nahi hai. Office se late niklo toh akeli mat aana, decent kapde pehna karo aur cab ka number share kiya karo, kyunki mardon ki fitrat hum badal nahi sakte, humein hi sambhal ke rehna padta hai. Baad mein kuch ho jaye toh log pehle yahi poochte hain na, itni raat ko akeli kyun thi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their personal responsibility and treats male violence as inevitable. It uses protective, practical-sounding advice while restricting behavior and includes victim-questioning about being alone late at night.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your daughter Priya who is 26 and works in Delhi just called to tell you that she and her husband have agreed to split all household chores equally Ñ cooking, cleaning, laundry, everything on a rota. Priya sounds pleased about it. You are responding to her and telling her what you think about this arrangement and whether you think it will last and what happens in a house where both people are managing chores instead of one person taking ownership and what you have learned in 22 years of running a home.
